step1_subtitle: Wählen Sie das mit Ihrem Raspberry Pi verbundene Relaisboard aus.
step3_subtitle: Aktivieren Sie jedes Relais für 3 Sekunden, um die physische Verbindung zu bestätigen.
step4_subtitle: Optional. Verbinden Sie sich mit Ihrem MQTT-Broker für die Home Assistant-Integration.
step4_toggle_desc: Stellt Zonen als Schalter in Home Assistant bereit
settings_reset_desc: Setzt das Board, Zonen, Zeitpläne und den Verlauf zurück.
settings_reset_confirm: Dadurch werden alle Zonen, Zeitpläne und der Verlauf gelöscht. Fortfahren?
smart_watering_desc: Bewässerung überspringen, wenn Regen vorhergesagt ist
smart_watering_map_hint: Karte antippen, um den Standort festzulegen
sw_status_no_loc: Kein Standort konfiguriert — Lat/Lon oben einstellen
settings_exclusive_desc: Nur eine Zone gleichzeitig aktiv. Das Aktivieren einer neuen Zone schaltet die aktuelle ab.
sched_passive_desc: Home Assistant verwaltet die Bewässerung. Interne Zeitpläne sind pausiert.
sched_days_error: Wählen Sie mindestens einen Tag aus.
mqtt_mode_active_desc: Sprinqua verwaltet eigene Zeitpläne. HA empfängt Statusaktualisierungen.
mqtt_mode_passive_desc: Interne Zeitpläne pausiert. HA hat vollständige Kontrolle über MQTT.
install_meta_desc: How to install Sprinqua on your Raspberry Pi running Orbit OS. Step-by-step guide: install the .orb package, open the web UI, and configure your relay board.
install_p: From a fresh Raspberry Pi to a working irrigation controller in under 10 minutes.
req_pi_p: Any model with GPIO pins — Pi 3, 4, 5 or Zero 2 W. Must be running Orbit OS.
req_relay_p: A GPIO-controlled relay HAT. Sprinqua supports 7+ boards out of the box — from 3 to 8 channels.
req_browser_p: Any modern browser on the same local network. No app install needed — Sprinqua is a web UI.
s1_h3: Install Orbit OS on your Raspberry Pi
s1_p: Download the Orbit OS installer and run it on your Raspberry Pi. It sets up the OS, the app runtime, and the store in one go. After installation, the device will automatically restart.
s1_skip: Already have Orbit OS with a Store account? Skip to step 4 →
s2_h3: Open the Orbit OS Launcher in your browser
s2_p: Open a browser on any device on the same network and navigate to your Raspberry Pi's address. The Orbit OS Launcher will open and ask for your username and password.
s3_h3: Add your Orbit OS Store account
s3_p: In the launcher, click the Settings icon, go to Account, and sign in with your Orbit OS Store account. This allows you to install apps from the store.
s3_card_p: Sign in with your Orbit OS Store credentials to enable app installation.
s4_h3: Install Sprinqua from the Orbit OS Store
s4_p: Open the Orbit OS Store and search for the Sprinqua application. Tap Install, select your device from the list, and confirm — Orbit OS will download and deploy it automatically.
s4_card_p: Find this icon in the store and tap it to open the app page.
s5_p: Go back to the Orbit OS Device Launcher at your device's address. Sprinqua is now installed — you will see its icon in the launcher. Tap it to open the app.
s5_card_p: The icon is now visible in your launcher. Tap it to open the app.
s6_p: On first launch, Sprinqua shows the setup wizard. It walks you through three quick steps:
s6a_p: Pick your exact board model from the list. Sprinqua auto-fills the correct GPIO pin mapping.
s6b_p: Assign a name and type (sprinkler, drip, mist…) to each relay channel. Set a safety auto-off limit per zone.
s6c_p: Pulse each relay for 3 seconds to verify the physical wiring before committing to the configuration.
s7_p: You're done. Turn zones on manually, create weekly schedules, enable Smart Watering to skip rainy days, or connect to Home Assistant via MQTT. All from the same clean UI.
ha_p: If you use Home Assistant, Sprinqua can publish all zones as MQTT switches with auto-discovery — no manual entity configuration needed.
ts_a1: Make sure your computer and the Raspberry Pi are on the same local network. Try using the device's IP address instead of the hostname. Check the Orbit OS dashboard to confirm Sprinqua is running.
ts_q2: Relay fires but nothing happens physically
ts_a2: Check that the relay board is powered (many boards need an external 5V supply in addition to GPIO). Verify that the COM/NO/NC wiring is correct for your valve type. The relay click sound confirms GPIO is working — the issue is downstream.
ts_a3: Make sure you selected the exact board model in the wizard. Different board revisions sometimes use different GPIO layouts. Use Settings → Re-run Hardware Wizard to reconfigure. If your board isn't listed, open a board request.
ts_a4: Confirm that Smart Watering is enabled in Settings and that your location coordinates are set. The check runs ~1 hour before a scheduled run. Open-Meteo requires internet access from the Raspberry Pi — verify the device can reach the internet.
cta_p: Open an issue on GitHub and we'll help you sort it out. Feature requests and board additions are also welcome.
